title: Coucou and flying fish
subtitle: Barbados national dish with cornmeal and okra
excerpt: Stiff cornmeal and okra stirred together to a thick, smooth polenta-like coucou, served alongside flying fish fillets quickly steamed or braised in a fragrant tomato, onion, and scotch bonnet sauce. Coucou and flying fish is the national dish of Barbados, the two elements are always served together, the soft coucou shaped into a dome on the plate alongside the glazed fish fillets.
sourceNotes: Coucou and flying fish is the national dish of Barbados. Coucou is derived from the West African fufu and turned corn pudding traditions brought by enslaved people. Flying fish (Hirundichthys affinis) is found in the warm waters around Barbados in abundance, Barbados is nicknamed 'The Land of the Flying Fish'. The combination of coucou and flying fish appears in Barbadian cooking literature from the 19th century.

content.text: Coucou requires constant stirring and strong arms. The cornmeal thickens quickly once it starts cooking and must be beaten vigorously with a long-handled spoon (the coucou stick, traditionally a long wooden paddle) to prevent lumps. The okra is cooked first and the liquid used in the coucou, the okra's natural mucilage helps produce the smooth, glossy finish.

Flying fish is widely available in the UK from Caribbean fishmongers and some supermarkets, especially in areas with a large Barbadian or Caribbean community. Mackerel fillets are the standard substitute in terms of flavour and texture. The fish is cooked quickly, the sauce is the foundation, and the fish needs only 5 to 6 minutes in it.

items.name: Flying fish
items.prepNote: filleted and boned, or mackerel fillets

content.text: Simmer the {{okra}} in 600 ml of lightly salted water for 10 minutes. Strain and reserve the okra water. In a heavy saucepan over medium heat, slowly pour the {{polenta}} into the reserved okra water, whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Add the {{sea-salt-fine}}. Reduce to low heat and stir vigorously with a wooden spoon for 15 to 20 minutes until the coucou pulls cleanly away from the sides of the pan and holds its shape when tested. Stir in the cooked okra and {{unsalted-butter}}. Cover and rest for 5 minutes. Shape into domes with a bowl.

Season the {{flying-fish}} with lime juice, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 4 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{tomato}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, and {{thyme}}. Cook for 3 minutes until the tomatoes soften. Add 100 ml of water, bring to a simmer, then lay the fish fillets in the sauce. Cover and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until the fish is cooked through. Serve the fish alongside a dome of coucou, spooning the sauce over both.

items.fix: Add the cornmeal in a very thin stream while whisking. If lumps form, beat them out vigorously. They can often be smoothed before the coucou fully sets
items.cause: Cornmeal added too fast or too much at once

items.fix: Use the lowest possible heat once the coucou has thickened and stir constantly. A non-stick pan helps
items.cause: Heat too high or too little stirring
items.symptom: Coucou gluey and sticking to the pan

items.fix: Flying fish and mackerel fillets cook in 5 to 6 minutes. Check by pressing gently, the flesh should just flake. Remove immediately when done

content.text: Coucou and flying fish is as central to Barbadian identity as any dish is to any national cuisine. The flying fish is on the Barbadian coat of arms; the coucou (brought from West Africa through the enslaved population) represents the African roots of Barbadian food culture. Together they form a dish that has been eaten in Barbados for centuries. Barbados is nicknamed 'The Land of the Flying Fish', the fish appears in the waters around the island in such abundance that it is effectively the island's signature protein. In Britain the dish is less commonly encountered outside the Barbadian diaspora, where it remains a Sunday staple.
